content.problemStatement:
Kushagra is an extremely successful man. He has achieved everything a person can aspire for. He has been invited to give a speech about time management. But really he is no expert in managing time. The secret behind his huge success are rather his clever hacks. Obviously, he plans to use another such hack to prepare an epic time management speech.

Kushagra's vocabulary consists of N\text{N}N words. He needs to write a speech (formally, a sequence of words with or without repetition) using exactly K\text{K}K of these words.

Goodness of any letter = Index of that letter in the alphabet (1 indexed)

Goodness of a word = Sum of goodness values of the letters in it

For example the word abc has goodness = 1+2+3 = 6

Goodness of a speech is calculated as follows.


The 1st word contributes the value same as its own goodness.
The ith word contributes, its goodness minus\text{minus}minus the goodness of its longest prefix which is shared with at least one of the j (j< i) strings already used in the speech.
For example, if K = 2 and the speech is (abc,abde), then the goodness will be goodness(abc)+goodness(de) = (1+2+3) + (4+5) = 15.

Calculate the goodness of the best speech that Kushagra can write.

###Input

The first line contains a single interger T\text{T}T - the number of testcases.

For each testcase,

The first line of input contains two space-separated integers, N\text{N}N and K\text{K}K.

The following N lines contain N distinct strings consisting of only lowercase letters of english alphabet.

###Output
For each testcase print one line containing the goodness of the best speech that Kushagra can write.

###Constraints
1≤T≤101 \leq \text{T} \leq 101≤T≤10

1≤N≤1051 \leq \text{N} \leq 10^51≤N≤105

1≤K≤1091 \leq \text{K} \leq 10^91≤K≤109

1≤1 \leq1≤ Sum of lengths of all N\text{N}N strings ≤105\leq 10^5≤105

###Sample Input
1
3 2
c
ab
abc

###Sample Output
9

###Explanation
One possible speech of maximum goodness can be S = (c, abc).
Goodness(S) = goodness(c) + goodness(abc) = 3 + (1+2+3) = 9
constraints: 1 <= Sum of lengths of all N strings <= 10^5
content.inputFormat: The first line contains a single integer T - the number of test cases. For each test case, the first line contains two space-separated integers, N and K. The following N lines contain N distinct strings consisting of only lowercase letters of English alphabet.
content.outputFormat: For each test case, print one line containing the goodness of the best speech that Kushagra can write.

sampleTestCases.explanation: One possible speech of maximum goodness can be S = (c, abc). Goodness(S) = goodness(c) + goodness(abc) = 3 + (1+2+3) = 9

solution.approach: To solve this problem, we need to calculate the goodness of each word and then find the best speech by selecting the words that maximize the total goodness. We can use a greedy approach to select the words. First, we calculate the goodness of each word and store it in a list. Then, we sort the list in descending order of goodness. Finally, we select the top K words from the sorted list and calculate the total goodness of the speech.
solution.timeComplexity: O(T * N * M * log(N)) - where T is the number of test cases, N is the number of words, and M is the maximum length of a word. The time complexity is dominated by the sorting of the words.
solution.spaceComplexity: O(T * N * M) - where T is the number of test cases, N is the number of words, and M is the maximum length of a word. The space complexity is dominated by the storage of the words and their goodness values.

code.Python3:
# COMPLETE, RUNNABLE Python3 code
import sys

def calculateGoodness(word):
    goodness = 0
    for char in word:
        goodness += ord(char) - ord('a') + 1
    return goodness

T = int(input())
while T > 0:
    N, K = map(int, input().split())
    words = [input() for _ in range(N)]
    goodness = [calculateGoodness(word) for word in words]
    goodness.sort(reverse=True)
    totalGoodness = sum(goodness[:K])
    print(totalGoodness)
    T -= 1
